codeReview.summary: The implementation correctly adds an upstream login token cache to APIServer with thread-safe double-checked locking. The getOrRefreshUpstreamClient method properly uses sync.RWMutex for concurrent read access and exclusive write access during refresh. All three call sites (coursesHandler, lecturesHandler, prepareJobRuntime) were updated to use the cached client. The implementation covers the core requirements: caching, reuse, auto-refresh on expiry, non-poisoning on login failure, and thread safety.

issues.description: The upstreamCacheEntry struct has a `mu sync.Mutex` field that is never used anywhere in the code. The comment says 'Protects concurrent refresh attempts' but the actual concurrency control is handled by the APIServer-level `upstreamCacheMu sync.RWMutex`. This dead field adds confusion and should be removed.

TestUpstreamCachePopulatedAfterFirstCall calls getOrRefreshUpstreamClient but ignores the error with `_, _, _ = s.getOrRefreshUpstreamClient(...)`. Since the config points to a fake URL, login will always fail and the cache will never be populated in this test. The test then checks `afterCache` but the assertions are inside a conditional `if afterCache != nil` block, meaning the test silently passes even though it verifies nothing when upstream is unavailable (which is always in CI). This test provides no real coverage.

TestUpstreamCacheReuseOnSubsequentCalls, TestUpstreamCacheExpiredTokenRefreshes, and TestUpstreamCacheConcurrentAccess all use t.Skipf when upstream login fails. Since CI environments won't have access to the real Impartus upstream, these tests will always be skipped. The tests should use dependency injection with a mock client instead of relying on real upstream availability. This is noted in the mission's AGENTS.md: 'Token cache tests: Use mock HTTP clients. Verify call counts on the mock.'

In prepareJobRuntime, the original code created a client with a custom HTTP timeout: `client.New(client.NewHTTPClient(parseHTTPTimeout(cfg.HTTPTimeout)), nil)`. The new code uses `getOrRefreshUpstreamClient` which always creates `client.New(nil, nil)` (default timeout). This means the cached client may not respect the per-job HTTPTimeout config. However, since the cache shares one client across all requests/jobs, per-job timeouts are inherently incompatible with caching. This is a reasonable tradeoff but worth noting.

The 23-hour hard-coded token expiry is a reasonable heuristic but isn't derived from any server-provided TTL. If the upstream server changes its token lifetime, this constant would need manual updating. A comment already acknowledges 'Token typically valid for 24h', which is acceptable documentation.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The upstreamCacheEntry struct has a `mu sync.Mutex` field that is defined but never used. The actual concurrency control is via the APIServer-level upstreamCacheMu (sync.RWMutex). The AGENTS.md coding conventions don't mention a pattern for cache struct design or when to use per-entry vs. top-level locks, which may have led to this confusion.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/server/server.go:289 — `mu sync.Mutex` field defined but never referenced (no `cached.mu` usage found anywhere)

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker ran into a nil pointer dereference during initial implementation (transcript shows cache tests failing, then a fix for trying to lock nil mutex). The final design uses the APIServer-level RWMutex instead of per-entry mutex. The architecture.md doesn't document the token caching pattern or the double-checked locking pattern used, which would help future workers understand the concurrency approach.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows: 'I see the issue. When the cache is nil, I'm trying to lock a nil mutex' — worker had to debug this at runtime. architecture.md has no section on caching patterns.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The go-backend-worker skill says 'Write failing tests BEFORE implementation' (Step 3: Red), but the transcript shows the worker wrote implementation first, then tests. The skill also says to use 'mock HTTP client for tests' but the worker wrote tests that depend on real upstream connectivity (using t.Skipf when unavailable). The skillFeedback.followedProcedure was reported as true, which seems inaccurate.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript: worker edited server.go first (implementation), then added tests after. All TestUpstreamCache* tests use t.Skipf when upstream login fails rather than mocking. handoff.skillFeedback.followedProcedure = true.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The 23-hour token cache TTL is a magic number that should be documented. The architecture.md doesn't mention the upstream token caching mechanism at all. The data flow section says 'Handler creates upstream client, logs in to Impartus' but should now say the client is cached and reused.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/server/server.go:625 — `expiresAt: time.Now().Add(23 * time.Hour)` with only an inline comment. architecture.md Data Flow section still describes per-request login.

summary: The upstream token cache implementation is correct and well-structured. The double-checked locking pattern (RLock fast path → Lock slow path → double-check) properly handles concurrent access and avoids thundering herd on expiry. All three handler sites (courses, lectures, jobs) correctly delegate to getOrRefreshUpstreamClient. The main concerns are: (1) a dead `mu sync.Mutex` field on upstreamCacheEntry that's never used, (2) tests that require real upstream connectivity and will be skipped in CI — the mission's own guidance says to use mock HTTP clients, and (3) the cached client uses default HTTP timeout instead of respecting per-job timeout config from prepareJobRuntime. These are all non-blocking issues that don't affect correctness of the core caching mechanism.
